lessons.title: Printing in Java
lessons.goal: Display text and numbers on the screen with System.out.println, and control formatting with escape sequences.

blocks.html: Every program needs to communicate with the person using it. In Java, the most basic way to send information to the screen is to print it. Almost every program you write will start by printing something, so this is where we begin.

Java code lives inside a class and runs starting from a special method called main. For now, treat the outer lines as a fixed frame you write your code inside — we will unpack them properly in Unit 3.
The important line is System.out.println("Hello, world!");. Read it as a command: "System, on your output, print this line." Three things to notice:

1. The text goes in double quotes. Anything inside "..." is a String — a piece of literal text — and Java prints it exactly as written. 2. Every statement ends with a semicolon ; — this is how Java knows one instruction has finished. 3. println adds a new line at the end, so the next thing printed starts fresh below.
There are two printing commands. println ("print line") moves to a new line afterward. print stays on the same line.

Some characters are hard to type directly inside a String — like a new line, or a quotation mark (which would look like the end of your text). Java solves this with escape sequences: a backslash \ followed by a letter that stands for a special character.

items: \n
New line — moves to the next line
\t
Tab — inserts horizontal spacing
A literal double-quote character
A literal backslash

blocks.out:
Line one
Line two
Name:	Ava
She said "hi"

blocks.html: You can print numbers directly (no quotes — they are values, not text), and you can join pieces together with the + operator, called concatenation when text is involved.

blocks.title: Why the parentheses around (3 + 4)?
blocks.html: Without them, "Sum: " + 3 + 4 reads left to right: it first joins the text to 3 (making "Sum: 3"), then joins 4 to that, giving Sum: 34. The parentheses force the addition to happen first. You will meet this rule again in 2.4.

blocks.title: Common beginner mistakes
blocks.html: Forgetting the semicolon — every statement needs one; leaving it off is the #1 cause of "it won't compile." Forgetting the quotes — System.out.println(Hello); makes Java look for a variable named Hello and fail. Using smart/curly quotes — copy-pasting from a document can insert " " instead of straight " ", which Java rejects.

items: What is the difference between print and println?
println moves to a new line after printing; print stays on the same line.
What does "a\nb" print?
Two lines: a then b. \n is a newline.
Why does "Sum: " + 3 + 4 print Sum: 34 instead of Sum: 7?
Java works left to right: text + number becomes text, so both numbers are glued on as characters. Use (3 + 4) to add first.

blocks.html: Print a three-line "business card": your name, a title, and an email — each on its own line. Use a single println with \n, then rewrite it using three separate println calls.

lessons.title: Variables and Types
lessons.goal: Store, name, and reuse values using variables, and choose the right type for each kind of data.

blocks.html: A variable is a named box in memory that holds a value you want to keep and reuse. Instead of writing the number 25 in ten places, you store it once in a variable and refer to it by name — and if it needs to change, you change it in one spot.
To make a variable you write its type, then its name, then (usually) an = and a starting value. This is called declaring and initializing.

Read int age = 17; as: "make an integer box called age and put 17 in it." The = is not "equals" in the math sense — it means assignment: "take the value on the right and store it in the box on the left."

blocks.text: The four types you need first

items: int
Whole numbers, positive or negative: -3, 0, 42. No decimal point.
double
Numbers with decimals: 3.14, -0.5, 2.0.
boolean
A truth value — exactly true or false. Nothing else.
String
Text, in double quotes: "hello". (Technically not a primitive, but you use it constantly.)

blocks.title: Also: char
blocks.html: A char holds a single character in single quotes: char grade = 'A';. You will see it occasionally; String covers most text needs.
Once declared, you can reassign a variable — but you only state the type once, when you first create it. After that, just use the name.

Names must start with a letter (or _/$), contain no spaces, and cannot be Java keywords like int. Beyond the rules, Java programmers follow camelCase: start lowercase, capitalize each new word — firstName, totalScore, isGameOver. Good names describe what the box holds.

blocks.title: Common beginner mistakes
blocks.html: Type mismatch: int age = "17"; fails — "17" is text (a String), not a number. Drop the quotes. Redeclaring: writing int score twice creates a conflict; only declare once. Using before assigning: a variable with no value can't be used yet — always give it a starting value. Putting a decimal in an int: int x = 3.5; fails because int holds whole numbers only.

items: Which type best fits a person's exact bank balance, like 42.50?
double — it has a decimal part. int would lose the cents.
What is wrong with boolean done = "false";?
The quotes make it a String. A boolean uses the bare word: boolean done = false;
After int n = 4; n = n + 1;, what is n?
5 — the right side is computed first (4 + 1), then stored back into n.

blocks.html: Declare variables for a product: a String name, an int quantity, and a double price. Print a line like 3 x Notebook @ 4.99 by concatenating them.
blocks.meta: checks: uses int, double, String; prints combined line

lessons.title: User Input
lessons.goal: Read what the user types using a Scanner, so your programs can respond to real input.

blocks.html: So far your programs only speak — they print. To listen, Java uses a tool called Scanner that reads what the user types at the keyboard. This is what turns a static program into an interactive one, like the guessing game.
Scanner lives in Java's library, so you must import it at the very top of your file, then create one connected to the keyboard (System.in).

blocks.out:
What is your name? Ava
Hello, Ava!

blocks.html: The pattern is always the same: print a prompt so the user knows what to type, then read their answer into a variable.
Which method you call depends on what you expect the user to type:

items: input.nextLine()
Reads an entire line of text as a String.
input.nextInt()
Reads a whole number as an int.
input.nextDouble()
Reads a decimal number as a double.
input.next()
Reads a single word (stops at the first space).

blocks.out:
Enter your age: 17
Next year you'll be 18

blocks.title: The famous nextLine() trap
blocks.html: When you call nextInt() or nextDouble() and then call nextLine() afterward, the nextLine() often reads an empty line and seems to "skip" the user. This is because nextInt() leaves the invisible Enter keypress behind, and nextLine() grabs it. The fix: add an extra input.nextLine(); right after nextInt() to swallow that leftover, before reading real text.

blocks.title: Where does input come from in the platform?
blocks.html: In the coding exercises and clean quizzes, "typed" input is supplied to your program automatically (the stdin field). Your job is to read it with Scanner exactly as if a person were typing.

items: Why do you print a prompt before reading input?
So the user knows what the program expects — otherwise it looks frozen.
You need to read a decimal like 3.5. Which method?
nextDouble().
What line do you add to avoid the nextInt-then-nextLine skip?
An extra input.nextLine(); after the nextInt() to consume the leftover newline.

blocks.html: Ask the user for two whole numbers and print their sum. Prompt before each read, and read with nextInt().
blocks.meta: stdin: two integers · checks: prints their sum

lessons.title: Arithmetic Expressions
lessons.goal: Compute with + − * / %, and avoid the traps of integer division and operator precedence.

blocks.html: Java does math with the operators you already know, plus one you might not: the remainder operator. The mechanics are simple, but two behaviours surprise nearly every beginner — so we will spend most of our time there.

items: Addition (or String joining)
Subtraction
Multiplication
Division
Modulus — the remainder after division

blocks.code:
System.out.println(7 + 3);   // 10
System.out.println(7 - 3);   // 4
System.out.println(7 * 3);   // 21
System.out.println(7 % 3);   // 1  (7 / 3 is 2 remainder 1)

blocks.html: When you divide two int values, Java gives an int back — it throws away any decimal part. It does not round; it truncates.

blocks.code:
System.out.println(7 / 2);       // 3, not 3.5
System.out.println(1 / 2);       // 0, not 0.5
System.out.println(7.0 / 2);     // 3.5  (one double is enough)

blocks.html: The rule: if both sides are int, you get int division. Make at least one side a double (write 7.0, or cast — see 2.5) to get a decimal result.
% answers "what's left over?" and it is more useful than it looks. n % 2 is 0 for even numbers and 1 for odd — the standard even/odd test. n % 10 gives the last digit of a number.
Java follows the usual math precedence: *, /, % happen before + and −. Use parentheses to force a different order or just to make your intent obvious.

blocks.code:
System.out.println(2 + 3 * 4);     // 14, not 20
System.out.println((2 + 3) * 4);   // 20

blocks.html: Updating a variable using its own value is so common that Java has shorthands. x += 5 means x = x + 5. And x++ adds exactly 1 — the workhorse of loops.

blocks.title: Common beginner mistakes
blocks.html: Expecting a decimal from int division: double avg = 5 / 2; stores 2.0, not 2.5, because 5 / 2 is computed as ints first. Write 5.0 / 2. Forgetting precedence: 1 + 2 * 3 is 7, not 9. When in doubt, add parentheses.

items: What is 9 / 4 in Java, and why?
2 — both are ints, so the .25 is discarded (integer division).
How do you test whether an int n is even?
n % 2 == 0 — even numbers leave no remainder when divided by 2.
What is 10 - 2 * 3?
4 — multiplication happens before subtraction.

blocks.html: Read an int number of total minutes and print it as hours and minutes — e.g. 135 → "2 hours and 15 minutes". Use / for hours and % for the leftover minutes.
blocks.meta: stdin: one integer · checks: correct hours and minutes

lessons.title: Casting
lessons.goal: Convert values between types on purpose — widening automatically, narrowing with a cast.

blocks.html: Sometimes you have a value of one type but need it as another — an int where a double is expected, or a precise division where integer math would ruin the answer. Converting between types is called casting.
Going from a "smaller" type to a "bigger" one — int into double — is safe (no information lost), so Java does it for you.

Going the other way — double into int — can lose the decimal part, so Java makes you ask for it on purpose by writing the target type in parentheses. Casting to int truncates (chops off the decimal), it does not round.

blocks.text: The most important use: fixing integer division

blocks.html: Remember from 2.4 that int / int loses the decimal. Casting one operand to double before dividing fixes it — this is where you will reach for casting most often.

blocks.title: Watch the order
blocks.html: (double) total / count casts total first, then divides — correct. But (double)(total / count) does the int division first (getting 3), then converts to 3.0. The position of the cast matters.

blocks.title: Common beginner mistakes
blocks.html: Expecting rounding: (int) 4.99 is 4, not 5 — casting chops, it doesn't round. To round, that's a different tool (Math.round). Casting too late: casting the result of int division ((double)(1/2) → 0.0) doesn't help; cast an operand before the division.

items: What is (int) 7.8?
7 — the cast truncates the decimal part.
Why is a cast required to put a double into an int, but not the reverse?
int → double loses nothing (safe, automatic); double → int can lose the decimal, so Java makes you confirm it.
How do you get 2.5 from int a = 5, b = 2;?
(double) a / b — cast before dividing so it's not integer division.

blocks.html: Read two integers and print their average as a decimal (e.g. 5 and 2 → 2.5). You will need a cast to avoid integer division.
blocks.meta: stdin: two integers · checks: decimal average

lessons.title: Booleans
lessons.goal: Work with true/false values and use them to represent yes/no facts in your programs.

blocks.html: A boolean is the simplest type in Java: it holds one of exactly two values, true or false. Booleans are how a program represents any yes/no fact — is the game over, is the input valid, did the user win — and they are the fuel that powers if statements and loops.
Notice: true and false are bare keywords — no quotes. "true" with quotes is a String, a completely different thing.
You rarely type true yourself. More often, a boolean is the result of a comparison (covered fully in 2.8). A comparison like age >= 18 evaluates to a boolean.
A common pattern is a flag: a boolean variable that remembers whether something has happened, and gets checked later.

blocks.title: Naming tip
blocks.html: Boolean variables read best when named as a yes/no question: isOpen, hasWon, gameOver. Then if (gameOver) reads like plain English.

blocks.title: Common beginner mistakes
blocks.html: Quoting the value: boolean b = "true"; is wrong — that's a String. Use boolean b = true;. Comparing to true needlessly: if (isOpen == true) works but is redundant — if (isOpen) says the same thing more clearly.

items: How many values can a boolean hold?
Exactly two: true or false.
What is the difference between true and "true"?
true is a boolean value; "true" is a String (text). They are not interchangeable.
What type does the expression 5 > 3 produce?
A boolean — in this case true.

blocks.html: Read an integer and store, in a boolean variable, whether it is positive. Print the boolean.
blocks.meta: stdin: one integer · checks: prints true/false correctly

blocks.html: Real decisions often depend on more than one fact: "if it's a weekday and it's not raining." Java combines boolean values with three logical operators.

items: AND — true only when both sides are true
OR — true when at least one side is true
NOT — flips true to false and false to true

blocks.html: Think of && as demanding: everything must be true. Think of || as forgiving: any one true is enough. A quick truth table:

blocks.code:
true  && true   -> true
true  && false  -> false
false && anything -> false

true  || anything -> true
false || false  -> false

blocks.html: ! reverses a boolean. It's useful for "if this is not true."

blocks.code:
boolean gameOver = false;
System.out.println(!gameOver);       // true — the game is NOT over

blocks.html: You can build bigger conditions. ! binds tightest, then &&, then || — but parentheses make your meaning clear and are always allowed.

blocks.title: Common beginner mistakes
blocks.html: Using a single & or |: Java has those too, but for combining conditions you almost always want the doubled && and ||. Writing math-style ranges: 0 < x < 10 does not work in Java. Write it as two conditions joined with AND: 0 < x && x < 10.

items: When is a && b true?
Only when both a and b are true.
When is a || b false?
Only when both are false.
How do you express "x is between 1 and 10 inclusive"?
x >= 1 && x <= 10 — two comparisons joined by AND.

blocks.html: Read an integer and print true if it is between 1 and 100 (inclusive) using a single boolean expression with &&.
blocks.meta: stdin: one integer · checks: range test

lessons.title: Comparison Operators
lessons.goal: Compare values with == != < > <= >= and learn why Strings need .equals.

blocks.html: To make decisions, a program compares values. Each comparison produces a boolean — true or false — which then feeds an if or a loop.

items: Equal to
Not equal to
<
Less than
>
Greater than
<=
Less than or equal to
>=
Greater than or equal to

blocks.code:
int a = 5, b = 8;
System.out.println(a == b);   // false
System.out.println(a != b);   // true
System.out.println(a < b);    // true
System.out.println(a >= 5);   // true

blocks.title: The biggest trap: = vs ==
blocks.html: A single = is assignment (put a value in a box). A double == is comparison (are these equal?). Writing if (x = 5) when you meant if (x == 5) is one of the most common beginner bugs. Comparison always uses two equals signs.

This one catches everyone. For Strings, == does not reliably check whether the text is the same — it checks whether they are the exact same object in memory, which often is not what you want. To compare the actual text, use the .equals method.

blocks.title: Rule of thumb
blocks.html: Numbers and booleans: compare with ==. Strings: compare with .equals(...). For case-insensitive text matching, use .equalsIgnoreCase(...).

blocks.title: One more: comparing doubles
blocks.html: Because decimals are stored approximately, 0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3 can be false. For beginner programs this rarely bites, but be aware that exact == on double is not always trustworthy.

items: What is the difference between = and ==?
= assigns a value; == compares two values and returns a boolean.
How should you check if a String variable s equals "quit"?
s.equals("quit") — not s == "quit".
What does 7 != 7 evaluate to?
false — 7 is equal to 7, so "not equal" is false.

blocks.html: Read a line of text and print whether it equals "java" (use .equals). Then read two integers and print whether the first is larger.
blocks.meta: stdin: a word, then two integers

lessons.title: For Loops
lessons.goal: Repeat code a known number of times with a compact for loop.

blocks.html: A loop repeats a block of code. When you know (or can compute) how many times you want to repeat — print 10 lines, count to 100, step through positions 0 to 9 — the for loop is the natural choice, because it keeps all its bookkeeping on one line.
The header for (init; condition; update) has three parts, separated by semicolons:

items: int i = 1
Initialization — runs once, before the loop starts. Creates the counter.
Condition — checked before each pass. While true, the body runs; when false, the loop ends.
Update — runs after each pass. Usually moves the counter toward the stopping point.

blocks.html: The order of events: init → check condition → (if true) run body → update → check condition → … The counter i is a normal variable; you can use it inside the loop, print it, do math with it.

blocks.title: Common beginner mistakes
blocks.html: Off-by-one errors: i < 5 runs for i = 0,1,2,3,4 (five times), while i <= 5 runs six times (0–5). Decide whether your end value should be included and pick < or <= accordingly. Semicolon after the header: for (...); with a stray semicolon makes the loop body empty — a silent, confusing bug. Using i after the loop: a counter declared in the header only exists inside the loop.

items: How many times does for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++) run?
4 times — i takes 0, 1, 2, 3, then stops at 4.
What are the three parts of a for header, in order?
Initialization; condition; update.
How would you count from 5 down to 1?
for (int i = 5; i >= 1; i--).

blocks.html: Read an integer n and use a for loop to print the numbers 1 through n, each on its own line.

lessons.title: While Loops
lessons.goal: Repeat code an unknown number of times — as long as a condition stays true.

blocks.html: A while loop repeats a block as long as a condition is true. Use it when you do not know in advance how many repetitions you need — "keep asking until the guess is right," "keep going until the user types quit." The condition is checked before each pass, so if it starts false, the body never runs.

blocks.text: The three things every while loop needs

blocks.html: A while loop has the same three parts as a for loop, just spread out: you must set up the variable before the loop, test it in the condition, and change it inside the body. Miss the last one and the condition never becomes false.

blocks.title: The infinite loop
blocks.html: If nothing inside the loop moves the condition toward false, the loop runs forever. The classic cause is forgetting the update — leaving out count++ means count stays 0 and count < 3 is always true. Whenever you write a while loop, ask: "what line in here will eventually make the condition false?"

blocks.text: for vs while — which to use

blocks.title: A simple guide
blocks.html: Use a for loop when you know the count ("do this 10 times", "for each position 0 to n"). Use a while loop when you loop until something happens and can't count the passes ahead of time (user input, a search, a game continuing). Anything a for loop can do, a while loop can too — for is just tidier for counting.
This is the pattern behind your final-exam guessing game: keep reading guesses while the guess is wrong.

items: When is a while loop's condition checked?
Before every pass, including the very first — so a false condition means zero runs.
What causes an infinite loop?
The condition never becomes false — usually because the loop variable is never updated inside the body.
Would you use for or while to repeat until the user types "quit"?
while — you don't know how many times ahead of time.

blocks.html: Write a countdown: read an integer n and use a while loop to print n down to 1, one per line.
blocks.meta: stdin: one integer · checks: prints n..1 descending

lessons.title: If Statements
lessons.goal: Make decisions with if, else if, and else, running different code for different situations.

blocks.html: An if statement lets a program choose what to do based on a condition. It is the point where code stops being a fixed recipe and starts reacting — to input, to values, to the state of the game.
The condition inside if (...) must be a boolean — something that is true or false. If it's true, the first block runs; otherwise the else block runs. The else is optional.

blocks.text: Choosing among several options: else if

blocks.html: To pick between more than two outcomes, chain conditions with else if. Java checks them top to bottom and runs the first one that's true, then skips the rest.

blocks.title: Order matters in a chain
blocks.html: Because Java takes the first true branch, order your conditions carefully. If you checked grade >= 70 first, an A would wrongly print "C" — it matches that condition before reaching the stricter one.

blocks.title: Common beginner mistakes
blocks.html: Using = instead of ==: if (x = 5) is a bug — that's assignment, not comparison. Use ==. Comparing Strings with ==: use .equals (see 2.8). A semicolon after the condition: if (x > 0); ends the if immediately, so the block below always runs — a silent bug. Forgetting braces on multi-line blocks: without { }, only the next single line is part of the if.

items: What kind of value must go inside if (...)?
A boolean — an expression that is true or false.
In an if/else-if chain, how many branches run?
At most one — the first whose condition is true; the rest are skipped.
Why might checking grade >= 70 before grade >= 90 give wrong results?
Because the first true branch wins — a 95 would match >= 70 first and never reach the A check.

blocks.html: Read an integer and print "negative", "zero", or "positive" using an if / else-if / else chain.
blocks.meta: stdin: one integer · checks: correct category

lessons.title: Loop-and-a-Half
lessons.goal: Handle "read input until a signal" using a loop that must run once before it can decide to stop.

blocks.html: Some loops have an awkward shape: you can't check the stopping condition until after you've done part of the work — like reading input. You have to read a value before you can ask "was that the quit signal?" This is the loop-and-a-half pattern, and it shows up constantly in input handling and games.
Imagine reading numbers until the user enters -1 (a sentinel — a special value that means "stop"). A plain while loop is clumsy here because the first read has to happen before the first check.
A clean approach: loop forever, then break out the moment you detect the stop signal. break immediately exits the nearest loop.

blocks.out:
Enter a number (-1 to stop): 4
Enter a number (-1 to stop): 6
Enter a number (-1 to stop): -1
Sum: 10

blocks.html: Read the structure: read → check for the sentinel → if it's the stop signal, break; otherwise, process it. The break sits in the middle of the loop body — that "half" pass is where the name comes from.

blocks.title: This is your guessing game
blocks.html: The final-exam guessing game is exactly this shape: loop forever → read a guess → if it's correct, break; otherwise tell them higher/lower and loop again. Recognizing the pattern is half the battle.

blocks.title: Common beginner mistakes
blocks.html: Processing the sentinel: make sure your break comes before you use the value, or the stop signal (like -1) gets added into your sum. Forgetting break entirely: while (true) with no break is an infinite loop. break vs continue: break exits the loop; continue skips to the next pass. Beginners sometimes mix them up.

items: What is a sentinel value?
A special input value that signals "stop" — like -1 or "quit".
What does break do?
Immediately exits the nearest enclosing loop.
Why must the sentinel check come before processing the value?
So the stop signal itself isn't treated as real data (e.g. added to a sum).

blocks.html: Keep reading integers and adding them to a running total until the user enters 0, then print the total. Use while (true) and break.
blocks.meta: stdin: integers ending with 0 · checks: correct sum

lessons.title: Short-Circuit Evaluation
lessons.goal: Understand how && and || stop early, and use it to write safer conditions.

blocks.html: When Java evaluates a combined condition, it is lazy in a useful way: it stops as soon as the answer is certain. This is called short-circuit evaluation, and once you know it, you can order conditions to prevent errors.

For && (AND): if the left side is false, the whole thing is already false, so Java doesn't even look at the right side. For || (OR): if the left side is true, the whole thing is already true, so the right side is skipped.

blocks.text: Why it matters: guarding against errors

blocks.html: The example above is the key idea. Dividing by zero crashes a program. By checking x != 0 first and joining with &&, you guarantee the risky 10 / x only runs when x is safe — because if x is 0, short-circuiting skips the division entirely. The order of your conditions becomes a safety tool.

blocks.out: empty or doesn't start with A

blocks.title: The rule to remember
blocks.html: Put the cheap or protective check first, and the risky one second, joined by &&. That way the risky part only runs when it's safe. This is one of the most practical habits in all of programming.

items: In a && b, when is b skipped?
When a is false — the result is already false.
In a || b, when is b skipped?
When a is true — the result is already true.
How does short-circuiting let you avoid dividing by zero?
Check x != 0 first with &&; if x is 0 the division on the right is never evaluated.

blocks.html: Read an integer d. Print "ok" only if d is not zero AND 100 / d is greater than 5 — ordering the conditions so the division is safe.
blocks.meta: stdin: one integer · checks: no crash on 0

lessons.title: De Morgan's Laws
lessons.goal: Rewrite negated conditions cleanly by distributing a ! across && and ||.

blocks.html: Sometimes you write a condition for the opposite case — "if it is not the case that both X and Y." Negating a combined condition can be confusing. De Morgan's Laws are two simple rules that let you push a ! inward and turn a tangled negation into something readable.

blocks.code:
!(a && b)   is the same as   !a || !b
!(a || b)   is the same as   !a && !b

blocks.html: In words: distribute the NOT to each part, and flip the operator — AND becomes OR, OR becomes AND. "Not (A and B)" means "not-A or not-B." "Not (A or B)" means "not-A and not-B."
Say a form is valid only when the name is filled in AND the age is at least 18. The invalid case is the negation:
Both say the same thing: the form is invalid if the name is missing or the person isn't an adult. The second version is often easier to read and reason about.

blocks.title: Why learn this?
blocks.html: Beyond simplifying code, De Morgan's Laws help you avoid a classic bug: incorrectly negating a condition. When you flip a condition, you must flip both the comparisons and the connector. !(x > 0 && x < 10) is x <= 0 || x >= 10 — note every piece changed.

blocks.title: Common beginner mistake
blocks.html: Forgetting to flip the operator: students often negate each part but leave && as &&. !(a && b) is not !a && !b — the AND must become OR. Change all three things: each condition and the connector.

items: Rewrite !(a || b) without the outer NOT.
!a && !b — distribute the NOT and turn OR into AND.
What is !(x >= 5 && x <= 10) as a positive condition?
x < 5 || x > 10 — flip each comparison and change AND to OR.
When you distribute a NOT across &&, what does && become?
OR (||).

blocks.html: A user may enter only if they have a ticket AND are over 12. Read the two facts and print, using De Morgan's form (!hasTicket || age <= 12), whether they must be turned away.

lessons.title: Strings
lessons.goal: Work with text as String values — building them, measuring them, and comparing them correctly.

blocks.html: A String is a sequence of characters — a piece of text like "hello" or "Ava123". You have used Strings since lesson 2.1; now we look at what you can actually do with them. (Unit 3 goes deeper into String methods; this is the foundation.)
You create a String with double quotes, and join Strings (or a String and a value) with + — concatenation.
Notice full.length() — a method that asks the String how many characters it holds (spaces count). The dot means "ask this String to do something." You will meet many more String methods in Unit 3.
Characters in a String have positions, numbered from 0. charAt(i) gives the character at position i.

blocks.title: Counting from zero
blocks.html: This trips up everyone at first: the first character is at position 0, so in "Java" (length 4) the valid positions are 0, 1, 2, 3. Position 4 does not exist and would cause an error. Zero-based counting returns constantly in Unit 5 with arrays.
Worth repeating because it matters so much: to check whether two Strings hold the same text, use .equals, not ==.

blocks.title: Strings don't change
blocks.html: A String is immutable — methods like these never alter the original; they return a new value. pw.toUpperCase() gives back "SECRET" but leaves pw as "secret" unless you store the result. Keep this in mind as you learn more String methods.

blocks.title: Common beginner mistakes
blocks.html: Comparing with ==: use .equals for text. Off-by-one on charAt: the last valid position is length() - 1, not length(). Expecting a method to modify the String: Strings are immutable — capture the returned value.

items: What does "hello".length() return?
What is the position of the first character in a String?
0 — positions are zero-based.
How do you correctly check if String s equals "done"?
s.equals("done").

blocks.html: Read a line of text and print its length and its first character. Then print whether it equals "hello" (case-insensitive).
blocks.meta: stdin: a line of text · checks: length, first char, equality
